full_response:
Step 1.  Write the Schwarzschild line element  
ds² = −(1−2GM/r) dt² + dr²/(1−2GM/r) + r²(dθ²+sin²θ dφ²).

Step 2.  Insert a negative mass  
Let M = −|M| < 0.  Then

f(r) ≡ 1 − 2GM/r = 1 + 2G|M|/r .

• For every positive r, f(r) > 1, so there is no horizon shielding the origin.  
• The metric coefficients still blow up at r → 0, so we must check whether this is a true curvature singularity or merely a bad choice of coordinates.

Step 3.  Examine a curvature invariant  
For Schwarzschild spacetime the Kretschmann scalar is (derived once in lecture notes)

K ≡ R_{abcd} R^{abcd} = 48 (GM)² / r⁶ .

Notice that K depends on M only through M².  Therefore the sign of M is irrelevant:

K = 48 G² |M|² / r⁶ .

Step 4.  Behaviour of K  
• For any r ≠ 0, K is finite (indeed smaller than in the positive–mass case, because f(r) > 1 enlarges the proper radius).  
• As r → 0, K → ∞ ∝ 1/r⁶.

Because a scalar (coordinate-independent) curvature invariant diverges, the point r = 0 is a genuine spacetime singularity.  No change of coordinates can remove it, and no smooth extension of the manifold through r = 0 is possible.

Step 5.  Interpretation  
Hence a “region V’’ with r < 0 cannot be attached to the negative–mass Schwarzschild solution; the manifold simply ends at the naked singularity r = 0.

\boxed{\;r = 0\; \text{remains a genuine curvature singularity even for negative-mass Schwarzschild}\; }  
Confidence: 88%
extracted_answer: \;r = 0\; \text{remains a genuine curvature singularity even for negative-mass Schwarzschild}\;
